metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `model.profile.supports_tools` is accessed for non-Anthropic providers: the runner passes these as plain strings to `Agent()`, not as model objects — specify whether the check instantiates a pydantic-ai model object from the string (e.g. `pydantic_ai.models.infer_model(resolved)`) before reading `.profile`, or guards the check to the Anthropic-object path only, or uses an alternative mechanism for string-based providers.
findings.finding: The `model.profile.supports_tools` capability check has a mechanism-correctness gap: on non-Anthropic providers (OpenAI, Gemini), `runner.py` passes the model as a plain string (e.g. `'openai:gpt-4o'`) to `Agent(model_string, ...)` — no model object is instantiated in runner scope at the point the plan describes the check. Strings have no `.profile` attribute, so `model.profile.supports_tools` would raise `AttributeError` on these paths. The plan does not specify how to obtain a pydantic-ai model object for string-based providers at check time.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; runner.py lines 224-225 (`resolved = _pai_model(cfg)` / `model = self._model_override or resolved`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step: after `resolved = _pai_model(cfg)`, when `self._model_override is None` and `tools` is non-empty, call `pydantic_ai.models.infer_model(resolved)` (or equivalent) to obtain a model object and then read `.profile.supports_tools`. Cache by `resolved` string as already planned. This avoids the string-has-no-profile issue. Alternatively, scope the check to providers where a model object is already in hand (the Anthropic path), and note this limitation explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify exactly which Python object `model.profile.supports_tools` is read from in `runner.py`'s execution flow (constructed `AnthropicModel`? the string? pydantic-ai's internal model registry?) and anchor the `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` cache key to that object's lifecycle — add a pydantic-ai source reference (e.g. `pydantic_ai/models/__init__.py` line) or a minimal repro snippet that confirms the attribute on each supported model class.
findings.finding: The plan's central unverified capability assertion — 'model.profile.supports_tools (a bool)' — is cited only from an inline experiment log with no external anchor, and the plan does not specify what Python object 'model' refers to at the check site in runner.py's flow. On the Anthropic path, the model object is constructed inside _build_retrying_anthropic_model (returns AnthropicModel); on other paths pydantic-ai resolves the model from a string internally. The cache is keyed by 'resolved model string', but the .profile check must be on a constructed model object — the mapping between string key and object is not specified, creating a real implementation ambiguity.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check' paragraph; Verified library behavior section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify exactly WHICH object `model.profile.supports_tools` is read from: name the pydantic-ai function/constructor call that produces a model object with `.profile` from a model string, confirm it works offline (no network call), and confirm `.supports_tools` exists on `AnthropicModel`, `OpenAIModel`, and `FunctionModel` (the test model type) — or specify a fallback when `.profile` is absent.
findings.finding: `model.profile.supports_tools` is asserted as a 'VERIFIED' pydantic-ai 1.107.0 signal, but it cannot be confirmed from any observable source in the repo (zero codebase hits) and the plan does not address the mechanistic gap: `_pai_model(cfg)` in runner.py returns a *string*, not a model object — reading `.profile.supports_tools` requires an instantiated model object that does not exist at the point the plan describes the check.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py
findings.suggested_fix: If pydantic-ai exposes `infer_model(resolved_string)` → model_object offline, document that call. Otherwise, read `model.profile.supports_tools` from the already-constructed `AnthropicModel` returned by `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`, after construction but before `run_sync` — and skip the check entirely on non-Anthropic paths where model-object construction is deferred.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the protocol for obtaining a `model.profile`-bearing object on non-Anthropic providers (e.g. call `pydantic_ai.models.infer_model(resolved_string)` before the check) and define the AttributeError/missing-profile fallback rule (fail-open with WARNING, or fail-closed) in the 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check' section.
findings.finding: The construction-time `model.profile.supports_tools` check has no specified protocol for non-Anthropic providers where `model` remains a plain string. In `runner.py`, for OpenAI/Gemini the model variable at the check point is the string from `_pai_model(cfg)` (e.g. `'openai:gpt-4o'`); only Anthropic builds a real model object via `_build_retrying_anthropic_model`. Calling `.profile.supports_tools` on a string yields `AttributeError`. The plan does not specify how to obtain a model object for non-Anthropic providers, nor does it specify an AttributeError fallback rule.
findings.location: What section — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; runner.py lines 224-225 (model assignment)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'To access `.profile`, resolve the model string to a pydantic-ai model object via `pydantic_ai.models.infer_model(resolved)` before the check. If `AttributeError` is raised (custom adapter), log a WARNING and skip the check (fail-open) — the cache entry is still written so the skip is also only-once.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the spy mechanism for `test_capability_check_cached`: either (a) extract the capability check into a named helper function (e.g. `_check_tool_capability(model_str, model_obj)`) that can be spy-patched, or (b) state that the test directly inspects `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set membership before/after two calls with the same model string and specify how the set is reset between test runs.
findings.finding: The `test_capability_check_cached` AC states the check is 'invoked once per model string, asserted by a spy' but never specifies what the spy intercepts or how caching is observable. The cache is a module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set — the only observable side-effect is whether the `model.profile.supports_tools` access (or the set.add) is called once vs twice. The plan names no intercept point (not the set operations, not the model construction, not a helper function) leaving the test mechanism structurally undefined.
findings.location: AC line `test_capability_check_cached`; What section — 'CACHE: a module-level set()'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract the check into `_check_tool_capability(resolved: str, model_obj) -> None` in `runner.py`; the test monkeypatches this function and asserts call count == 1 after two runner invocations with the same model string. The module-level set is cleared in the test fixture via `runner_mod._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the spy/monkeypatch strategy for `test_usage_limits_on_run_not_constructor`: name the exact intercept point (e.g. 'monkeypatch `runner._import_pydantic_ai` to return a `MagicMock` subclass of `Agent` that records `__init__` and `run_sync` kwargs') so the test can be implemented without guesswork.
findings.finding: The `test_usage_limits_on_run_not_constructor` AC specifies a spy that asserts 'the `Agent(...)` constructor kwargs do NOT include `usage_limits`' but never specifies the interception mechanism. In `runner.py`, `Agent` is imported locally inside `run()` via `_import_pydantic_ai()`, making it non-trivially monkeypatchable — the plan doesn't specify whether to patch `pydantic_ai.Agent.__init__`, `runner._import_pydantic_ai`, or another seam. This is a missing processing-protocol specification co-located with the AC.
findings.location: AC line `test_usage_limits_on_run_not_constructor`; runner.py line 174 (`Agent = _import_pydantic_ai()`)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'Use `monkeypatch.setattr(runner_mod, "_import_pydantic_ai", lambda: MockAgent)` where `MockAgent` wraps a real `FunctionModel`-backed Agent and records constructor and `run_sync` kwargs — the same spy pattern already used in `test_cache_model_settings_attached_only_for_anthropic`.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define per-mode what 'non-empty result' means for the usage-plausibility WARNING (e.g. `text`: result string has len > 0 after strip; `findings`/`structured`: the raw output string from the agent has len > 0) so the AC for `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks` is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The usage-plausibility WARNING condition references 'a non-empty result' but never structurally defines what 'non-empty' means for each output mode. `runner.py` supports three modes: `text` (result is a string), `findings` (result has a `findings` list), and `structured` (arbitrary schema). 'Non-empty' is ambiguous across these — an empty findings list `[]` is a valid zero-finding review; a non-empty string `' '` might be whitespace-only; a structured dict may have empty values.
findings.location: What section — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING'; AC line `test_usage_plausibility_warns_never_blocks`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `model_override` concretely in the plan (e.g., name the Python variable/kwarg/flag in `runner.py` that signals a test-override model) so the executor can implement the carve-out without guessing.
findings.finding: The construction-time capability check carve-out 'not the `model_override` test path' references a rebar-specific concept that is never defined in the plan. An executor implementing the check in `runner.py` has no specification of what constitutes the `model_override` test path, how to detect it, or what Python symbol/flag to check.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check': 'FOR A TOOL-USING op only (the `tools` list is non-empty AND not the `model_override` test path)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: 'The `model_override` test path is identified by the boolean kwarg `model_override: bool = False` (or the presence of `MODEL_OVERRIDE` env var) passed to the runner; when `True`, skip the capability check entirely.'

findings.finding: The usage-plausibility telemetry warning never defines the threshold for 'zeroed/implausible', how to distinguish a genuinely zeroed provider adapter from the existing defensive empty-dict fallback in _extract_usage (which already silently returns {} for missing usage), or which logger/channel receives the warning.
findings.location: ## What ('A usage-plausibility telemetry warning (never a block) when per-provider usage looks zeroed/implausible') / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 3)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: 'The usage-plausibility warning fires in _extract_usage or at the call site in runner.py when: model_override is None (real provider path), execution_mode is agentic, and both input_tokens and output_tokens are 0. It emits logger.warning(...) and never raises. It is suppressed on the model_override/test path.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact attribute used to resolve the cache key (e.g., `model.model_name`) so the implementation and its test use the same stable identifier.
findings.finding: The cache key 'keyed by the resolved model string' does not specify how to resolve the string from the model object (e.g., `model.model_name`, `str(model)`, `model.name`). This is a defaultable gap: an executor will pick an approach, but if it diverges from the tested behavior (e.g., `str(model)` returns `<OpenAIModel object>` rather than `'gpt-4o'`), the cache may never hit across calls.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check': 'CACHE: a module-level `set()` `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` keyed by the resolved model string'
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'The cache key is `model.model_name` (the `str` attribute exposed by all pydantic-ai model objects in 1.107.0).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (e.g., `test_capability_check_skipped_on_model_override`) that asserts the construction-time LLMConfigError is NOT raised when the model_override test path is active, covering the exclusion rule described in What.
findings.finding: The 'model_override test path' exclusion described in What ('AND not the model_override test path') is a distinct behavioral rule — the construction-time check must NOT fire on that path — but no AC verifies this exclusion. An implementer following only the ACs could omit the exclusion entirely, breaking test-time usage of non-tool-calling model overrides.
findings.location: What section ('AND not the `model_override` test path'); AC: `test_capability_check_fails_fast` + `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '- [ ] Test: when `model_override` is in effect, `LLMConfigError` is NOT raised even if `model.profile.supports_tools` is False (`test_capability_check_skipped_on_model_override`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pytest fixture (or `monkeypatch`) in `test_agent_build_invariants.py` that clears `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` before each test that exercises the capability check, ensuring test isolation for the module-level cache.
findings.finding: The module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set is process-global mutable state; the plan's test for caching (`test_capability_check_cached`) does not address resetting this set between test runs, risking test-order contamination (a model string cached by one test silently skips the check in a later test that expects it to fire).
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_cached` / Files: `src/rebar/llm/runner.py`
findings.suggested_fix: In the test file, add a `@pytest.fixture(autouse=True)` that does `runner._TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED.clear()` (or equivalent monkeypatch) before each test in the capability-check test class/module.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error handling (try/except AttributeError → raise LLMConfigError with a clear message) for the `model.profile.supports_tools` access so custom adapters and future API changes surface a diagnostic error, not a raw AttributeError.
findings.finding: The construction-time `model.profile.supports_tools` attribute access has no specified error handling for AttributeError or similar exceptions that would arise from custom model adapters, mock models, or future pydantic-ai API changes that rename/remove the attribute — leaving an opaque crash instead of a clear, actionable error.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check'; Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the attribute access: `try: supports = model.profile.supports_tools except AttributeError: raise LLMConfigError('model.profile.supports_tools unavailable — verify pydantic-ai>=1.107.0 and adapter compatibility') from None`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address the TOCTOU race on `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` by either using a `threading.Lock` to guard the check-then-add, using `set.add` idempotently and accepting rare double-checks (and documenting this), or noting explicitly in the plan that double-firing is acceptable and harmless.
findings.finding: The module-level `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` set has a check-then-act (TOCTOU) race: two threads can both evaluate `model_key not in _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` as True before either inserts, causing the capability check to run more than once — violating the plan's 'once per unique model per process' guarantee and potentially masking double-initialization side effects.
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'CACHE: a module-level set() _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED keyed by the resolved model string'; Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py
findings.suggested_fix: Either wrap with a module-level lock (`_TOOL_CAP_LOCK = threading.Lock()`) around the check-and-add, or — if double-checking is truly harmless — document it explicitly and drop the 'ONCE' guarantee language to avoid misleading future maintainers.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC (and corresponding test, e.g. `test_capability_check_skipped_on_model_override`) asserting that the construction-time check is NOT invoked — and does NOT raise — when the model_override flag/path is active, even for a tool-using op.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that the construction-time capability check must NOT run on the `model_override` test path, but no AC or test is defined to verify this exclusion. If the exclusion logic is wrong, all existing tests that pass a model override will start raising LLMConfigError.
findings.location: 'What (concrete mechanisms)' — 'Construction-time provider tool-calling check' bullet; Acceptance Criteria — `test_capability_check_fails_fast` + `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '[ ] Test: when the model_override path is active, the construction-time supports_tools check is skipped and no LLMConfigError is raised even if supports_tools==False (`test_capability_check_skipped_on_model_override`).`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or justify the `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` process-level cache: either cite a profiling result showing the attribute read is measurably costly in a hot loop, or drop the cache and call `model.profile.supports_tools` inline at construction time (the check already happens at most once per `PydanticAIRunner.__init__` call, which is itself already bounded by gate-op construction).
findings.finding: The `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` module-level set is a premature optimization: the plan names 'a hot loop' as the target but cites no profiling result, call-rate measurement, or scale estimate. The underlying operation being cached (`model.profile.supports_tools` attribute read) is a single O(1) bool access — inherently negligible cost. The cache adds mutable process-lifetime state that risks cross-test pollution in a shared pytest process without extra setup/teardown, to avoid a cost that has never been measured as problematic.
findings.location: What section — 'CACHE: a module-level set() _TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED keyed by the resolved model string'
findings.suggested_fix: Drop `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` entirely. The construction-time check already runs once per `PydanticAIRunner` construction (not per `run()` call), so no process-level deduplication is needed. If the concern is multiple runners sharing the same model string, document it as a non-issue rather than caching it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For the `test_expected_tools_offered` AC, extend the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` (or add a sibling in `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py`) for the `filesystem_tools` name-check rather than re-asserting `rebar_tools` names in the new file; call out explicitly that `rebar_tools` name coverage already exists.
findings.finding: The plan's `test_expected_tools_offered` AC partially duplicates the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` test (line 274 of `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py`), which already asserts `__name__` membership for both `rebar_tools` configurations. The plan does not acknowledge this existing coverage or propose extending it, risking a redundant assertion for the rebar-tools portion.
findings.location: AC: 'Test: pai_tools.filesystem_tools/rebar_tools offer exactly the expected tool names — fails if a tool is stripped (test_expected_tools_offered)'
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_agent_build_invariants.py`, scope `test_expected_tools_offered` to only the `filesystem_tools` side (names `read_file`, `list_directory`, `search_files`). For rebar_tools, add a cross-reference comment pointing to the existing `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege` rather than duplicating it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify `test_expected_tools_offered`: if the intent is to guard against #5177 (post-construction tool stripping), the test must introspect the constructed `Agent` object's tool list (using the introspectable surface noted in the experiment log) rather than only checking builder `__name__`s — or explicitly restate the threat as 'builder regression only' and remove the #5177 claim.
findings.finding: The plan's `test_expected_tools_offered` AC specifies checking that `pai_tools.filesystem_tools`/`rebar_tools` 'return callables whose `__name__`s are exactly the expected set', but the stated threat it guards against is pydantic-ai #5177: 'a hook returns None [and] strips a tool' — a post-construction Agent-internal event, not a builder-output failure. The builder `__name__` test cannot detect tools stripped after the Agent is constructed. Yet the plan's experiment log asserts `Agent.__init__ has tools/toolsets: True (tools offered via the constructor; introspectable)`, implying Agent-level introspection is available and could actually verify tools are offered to the Agent. The AC and the threat model are mismatched: the specified test does not guard against the cited failure mode.
findings.location: What section — 'Tests — tools actually offered'; AC line `test_expected_tools_offered`; Verified library behavior — 'Agent.__init__ has tools/toolsets: True'
findings.suggested_fix: Use `Agent(model, tools=pai_tools.filesystem_tools(repo_path), ...).` and introspect the Agent's registered tool names (e.g. via the `tools` attribute or equivalent pydantic-ai 1.107.0 API) to assert the full expected set is present — this is the test that actually catches #5177.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the plan (or the test file spec) an explicit requirement that `test_capability_check_cached` and `test_capability_check_fails_fast` reset `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` (e.g. via `monkeypatch.setattr` or a fixture that clears the set) to ensure test-order independence.
findings.finding: The plan adds a `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` module-level cache set to `runner.py`, but does not enumerate that this process-lifetime mutable module state introduces a test-isolation hazard: any test that triggers the check for a model string will permanently mark it as checked for all subsequent tests in the same process, potentially masking a test that expects the check to run again.
findings.location: What section: 'CACHE: a module-level `set()` `_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED` keyed by the resolved model string' + AC: `test_capability_check_cached`
findings.suggested_fix: In the test, add: `monkeypatch.setattr(runner_mod, '_TOOL_CAPABILITY_CHECKED', set())` before each capability-check test.

findings.finding: The plan promises a construction-time provider tool-calling capability check ('the configured provider supports tool-calling for tool-using ops') but neither the plan text nor the experiment log identifies what pydantic-ai API surfaces this property. pydantic-ai 1.107.0 has no documented `Model.supports_tool_calling` or equivalent; detecting it typically requires either a trial call or class-name inspection — both of which have significant drawbacks.
findings.location: AC line 2 ('A construction-time capability check fails fast'); 'What' section bullet 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the correct attribute to inspect on the objects returned by `filesystem_tools`/`rebar_tools` (e.g., `.name` on `Tool` instances vs `.__name__` on raw callables) so `test_expected_tools_offered` actually exercises the right field and can catch a #5177-style strip.
findings.finding: The plan states tool builders 'return callables whose `__name__`s are exactly the expected set', but pydantic-ai tool builders return `Tool` wrapper objects (not raw callables), whose registered name is accessed via a `.name` attribute, not `.__name__`. The experiment log itself lists them as a bracketed list `[read_file, list_directory, search_files]`, consistent with `Tool` objects. If the test asserts `obj.__name__` on `Tool` instances, it will either raise `AttributeError` or silently compare the wrong field — making `test_expected_tools_offered` unable to catch the #5177 regression it is designed to detect (fixture invalidity, VCTD item 4).
findings.location: What (concrete mechanisms) — 'Tests — tools actually offered'; AC: `test_expected_tools_offered`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'callables whose `__name__`s' with 'Tool objects whose `.name` attributes' (or whichever attribute pydantic-ai 1.107.0 exposes) and update the AC to read: `assert {t.name for t in pai_tools.filesystem_tools(repo_path)} == {'read_file', 'list_directory', 'search_files'}`. Verify the attribute name against `Tool.__init__` or `Tool.name` in the pydantic-ai 1.107.0 source.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document a rollback/escape-hatch mechanism for the new construction-time LLMConfigError (e.g., an env-var bypass, a feature flag, or an explicit 'revert by removing the capability check in runner.py' note) so the change is reversible without a full code revert.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new LLMConfigError raise on the construction path that previously succeeded silently, changing observable behavior for any caller that currently constructs a tool-using agent with a non-tool-calling provider. The plan justifies the change but provides no rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract sequence, violating T4's requirement that acknowledgement alone is insufficient — a reversibility mechanism must be stated.
findings.location: What section ('assert model.profile.supports_tools is True; else raise LLMConfigError'); Scope section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line escape hatch: e.g., 'REBAR_SKIP_TOOL_CAP_CHECK=1 disables the construction-time assert; intended only for emergency rollback while a provider configuration issue is diagnosed.' Document this in the plan's Scope section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or CLAUDE.md note capturing the decision to shift build invariants to construction-time + process-lifetime cache (with the pydantic-ai issue refs: #5177, #6186, #1987, #5360) so the rationale outlives the ticket.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision (shifting build invariants from per-call to construction-time + a process-lifetime module-level cache) but does not include an update to any persistent design artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc).
findings.location: ## Scope / Files section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the plan title (or add a subtitle) to reflect that runner.py also gains a per-run telemetry warning, e.g. 'Agent-build invariants as tests + one-time capability check + usage-plausibility warning'.
findings.finding: The plan title promises 'tests + one-time capability check' but the body also permanently adds a per-run telemetry warning to runner.py that fires on every successful run — neither a test nor a one-time check. The title under-represents the scope of the behavioral change to runner.py.
findings.location: Title vs. 'What (concrete mechanisms)' section — 'Usage-plausibility telemetry WARNING' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Rename to: 'Agent-build invariants as tests, one-time capability check, and per-run usage-plausibility warning' — or add a one-line summary at the top of the body making the three deliverables explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `test_expected_tools_offered` (for both `filesystem_tools` and `rebar_tools` tool names) should live in the new `test_agent_build_invariants.py` file OR extend the existing `test_pydantic_ai_runner.py`, and explicitly note which existing tests (e.g. `test_rebar_tools_are_least_privilege`) are superseded or complemented.
findings.finding: The plan's new test file `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py` overlaps with existing coverage in `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py` (which already tests `rebar_tools.__name__` membership at line 277 and tool-count invariants). The plan does not enumerate whether these overlapping tests should be consolidated into the existing file or whether the new file supersedes/complements them — leaving implementors to potentially duplicate or conflict with existing test coverage.
findings.location: Files section: `tests/unit/test_agent_build_invariants.py` (new) vs existing `tests/unit/test_pydantic_ai_runner.py`

findings.finding: The test asserting 'the expected tools are actually offered to the agent' never defines what 'expected tools' means: which tool names, for which RunRequest configuration, and which pydantic-ai introspection API to query — so an implementor cannot write this test without guessing.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 1: 'Tests fail if the expected tools are not offered')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: 'The tools-offered test constructs a PydanticAIRunner with a known RunRequest (agentic mode, allow_comment=False) and asserts that `{"read_file", "list_directory", "search_files", "show_ticket"} ⊆ {t.name for t in agent._function_tools}` using pydantic-ai 1.107.0\'s `Agent._function_tools` attribute.' Confirm the attribute name via the experiment harness.

findings.finding: The construction-time capability check ('cached, invoked once per model, not per call') references a caching mechanism and a 'mis-provisioned provider' concept whose vocabulary (cache key, cache mechanism, what attribute to inspect, what exception to raise) is never defined, making the spec unimplementable as written.
findings.location: ## What / ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 2: 'A construction-time capability check fails fast (cached, invoked once per model, not per call)') / Files: src/rebar/llm/runner.py

findings.finding: The plan specifies 'a construction-time capability check that fails fast on a mis-provisioned provider for a tool-using op' but does not identify which pydantic-ai 1.107.0 API provides an OFFLINE query of whether a given provider/model supports tool-calling. The experiment log confirms tool_timeout and tools exist on Agent.__init__, but says nothing about a provider capability introspection API.
findings.location: ## What — 'A one-time capability assertion at model construction' / src/rebar/llm/runner.py (construction-time check)

findings.finding: The plan never defines the threshold for 'usage looks zeroed/implausible', leaving the executor to invent a definition. This is a defaultable gap that should be stated explicitly (e.g., 'warn when both prompt_tokens and completion_tokens are 0 after a non-trivial call').
findings.location: What section: 'A usage-plausibility telemetry warning (never a block) when per-provider usage looks zeroed/implausible' and AC3: 'Usage-plausibility emits a telemetry warning and never blocks a gate.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Triggers when `usage.request_tokens == 0 and usage.response_tokens == 0` on a response with non-empty output; verified by a unit test that patches the provider adapter to return zeroed usage and asserts a WARNING-level log entry is emitted.'

findings.finding: AC2 claims the construction-time capability check is 'cached, invoked once per model, not per call', but no described step or test proves the once-per-model property — `make test` alone does not verify invocation count.
findings.location: AC: 'A construction-time capability check fails fast (cached, invoked once per model, not per call).'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the test plan: 'A unit test patches the internal capability-probe function, constructs the same model twice, and asserts the probe was called exactly once (functools.lru_cache or equivalent). Verified via `make test`.' Also clarify the cache key (model class? model name string?) in the plan.

findings.finding: The usage-plausibility telemetry warning lacks a trigger condition, making the implementer's threshold choice arbitrary and likely to produce false positives (e.g., warning on every single_turn call where `requests=0` is a legitimate and expected value, not an indicator of a zeroed-usage provider bug).
findings.location: ## What / Acceptance Criteria (AC line 3: 'Usage-plausibility emits a telemetry warning and never blocks a gate')

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'testable introspection' for 'tools actually offered to the agent' but does not identify the specific pydantic-ai API surface used to inspect which tools an Agent was constructed with. Agent._function_tools is private; no public `agent.tools` or equivalent is documented in pydantic-ai 1.107.0. Without naming the introspection mechanism the test cannot be written correctly.
findings.location: Verified library behavior section / AC line 1 ('Tests fail if the expected tools are not offered')
findings.suggested_fix: Use a FunctionModel callback that receives AgentInfo (which has .function_tools) as the introspection vehicle — the existing test_runner_pydantic_budget.py already uses this pattern (AgentInfo.function_tools[0].name at line 35). Document this as the chosen seam in the plan.

findings.finding: AC3 ('Usage-plausibility emits a telemetry warning and never blocks a gate') describes an observable behavioral guarantee but no test deliverable is described anywhere in the plan to verify it — the What section's test bullet only covers tools-offered, usage_limits mis-placement, and provider tool-calling; the usage-plausibility warning is listed as a separate artifact (not a test subject). This leaves AC3 unverifiable by the described deliverables.
findings.location: AC3; What (bullet 3: 'A usage-plausibility telemetry warning (never a block)'); Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the What section's test bullet to include: 'the usage-plausibility path emits a warning and does not raise' — and add a corresponding test file/function in tests/unit/ that monkeypatches a zeroed-usage provider response and asserts a warning is logged (e.g., using pytest's caplog or a mock telemetry emitter).

findings.finding: The plan asserts a 'testable introspection' path for detecting tools-actually-offered (pydantic-ai #5177 hook-returns-None scenario), but the experiment log does NOT name the specific pydantic-ai 1.107.0 attribute or API used to read back the Agent's registered tool list post-construction. If the introspection relies on a private/internal attribute (e.g. `Agent._tools`, `Agent._toolsets`), a minor version bump could silently break the test without a CI failure on the application path itself.
findings.location: ## Verified library behavior (experiment log) / ## Acceptance Criteria: 'Tests fail if the expected tools are not offered'
findings.suggested_fix: In the experiment log or plan body, record the exact expression used (e.g. `Agent(model, tools=tools)._toolsets` or `list(agent.iter_tools())`) and verify it is a public/documented attribute. If only a private attribute is available, note it and add a version-sentinel assertion (e.g. `assert pydantic_ai.__version__.startswith('1.')`) next to the introspection call.

findings.finding: The 'cached, invoked once per model, not per call' specification for the construction-time capability check does not define the cache mechanism or its scope, leaving the implementation free to use a per-instance check (equivalent to per-call if instances are per-call) rather than a module-level LRU keyed on model string — which would silently violate the stated 'not per call' invariant.
findings.location: ## Acceptance Criteria (AC line 2: 'A construction-time capability check fails fast (cached, invoked once per model, not per call)')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Cache via a module-level `@functools.lru_cache` keyed on the resolved model-string prefix (e.g., `anthropic`, `openai`, `google-gla`), called from `PydanticAIRunner.__init__` or `preflight()`; the cache is process-scoped so repeated construction of a runner for the same model string pays the check once.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
